provider availability and utilization state across configured harnesses/providers.\n\u003c/context-digest\u003e\nDefine the server-side agent dashboard that lets operators inspect configured providers and make informed execution decisions.\n\n## Goals\n- Show configured providers/harnesses, effective routing availability, auth/health state, and current quota or headroom when known\n- Show utilization and performance signals already normalized by DDx: token usage, cost when available, recent latency/success, burn estimates, and freshness timestamps\n- Support search/sort/filter/reporting across provider, model, profile, status, and time window\n- Distinguish provider-native facts from DDx-derived estimates and from unknown values\n\n## Required spec surfaces\n- Clarify how live provider availability, cached quota snapshots, and historical usage are presented without inventing certainty\n\n## Required implementation planning\n- Define sample/fixture data needed to Playwright-test the dashboard states\n- Define how the dashboard relates to worker progress, execution history, and routing diagnostics without collapsing them into one .agents/skills/.ddx/beads.jsonl:388:{"acceptance":"1. .ddx/config.yaml schema supports profile_ladders (per-profile ordered tier list) AND the legacy profile_priority is still readable with a deprecation warning.\n\n2. Four shipped profile ladders: default=[cheap,standard,smart], cheap=[cheap], fast=[fast,smart], smart=[smart]. Documented in FEAT-006.\n\n3. Tier escalation in routing.go / execute_bead_orchestrator.go iterates profile_ladders[P] on failure, respecting --provider affinity (ddx-3c5ba7cc) and capability gating (ddx-4817edfd). Escalation triggers: execution_failed, land_conflict, post_run_check_failed, structural_validation_failed. no_changes uses the existing cooldown path.\n\n4. Bead routing event records: requested_profile, requested_tier, resolved_tier, escalation_count, final_tier. A run that escalates cheap -\u003e standard -\u003e success at standard has an event with escalation_count=1, resolved_tier=standard, final_tier=standard.\n\n5. 'ddx work --profile default' drains the queue by trying local first and escalating only when needed. Measured outcome over a test run of 10 representative beads shows at least 50% closing at the cheap tier (target: most work lands locally; escalations are the exception).\n\n6. Regression tests cover each ladder path: success-at-cheap, escalate-cheap-to-standard, escalate-all-tiers-then-exhaustion, --max-tier caps the ladder, --profile cheap never escalates.\n\n7. FEAT-006 Profile Routing section updated with the ladder semantics and the intent table (default/cheap/fast/smart).","closing_commit_sha":"93f938c41c93be78fc199bd150f455add6575d24","created_at":"2026-04-18T02:51:49.512908486Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-bbb65768","depends_on_id":"ddx-2d974641","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-18T02:52:01Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-bbb65768","depends_on_id":"ddx-e7254128","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-20T13:58:09Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-bbb65768","depends_on_id":"ddx-7bc0c8d5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-20T14:36:21Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-bbb65768","depends_on_id":"ddx-ea7f041c","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-21T14:36:16Z"}],"description":"## Target UX\n\nThe user-facing profile vocabulary (cheap / default / fast / smart) should map to concrete routing policies that take full advantage of local models without blocking on their limits. Specifically:\n\n### default profile (the common case)\nTry local models first, escalate to cloud if:\n- local model's context window is too small for the bead prompt\n- local model lacks a capability the bead requires (tools, structured output, etc.)\n- local dispatch fails (provider unreachable, timeout, model-side error)\n- bead reopens due to post-run check failure (review-malfunction, land-conflict, etc.)\n\nEscalation target: next tier up in the ladder (standard cloud — e.g. codex gpt-5.4 within subscription). If THAT fails, escalate once more to smart cloud. Then stop and unclaim.\n\n### cheap profile\nLocal only. Never escalate. Intended for high-volume exploratory work where cost dominates and adequate-quality is sufficient. If the cheap tier can't serve the bead, the bead unclaims and waits.\n\n### fast profile\nCloud-first, skip local entirely. Intended for latency-critical or interactive work where local inference's 9s warmup is unacceptable. Starts at a fast cloud model (e.g. gemini-flash or haiku) and escalates to smart on failure.\n\n### smart profile\nCloud-high-quality from the start. Intended for bead-review, adversarial checks, or beads whose AC demands high-quality reasoning. No escalation — if smart fails, the work fails meaningfully.\n\n## Why this matters (dogfooding signal)\n\nAs of 2026-04-18, DDx's default is cheap = qwen/qwen3.6 local (vidar-omlx). With v0.3.14 shipping the SSE-comment-frame filter, the local path actually works end-to-end. The queue can drain mostly on local hardware for pennies per bead, escalating only when the bead genuinely needs cloud quality. That's the throughput-per-dollar goal (goal:throughput-per-dollar label).\n\n## Prereqs (already filed under ddx-2d974641 or sibling beads)\n\nThis epic is blocked on the autorouting epic delivering its exit-gate UX — 'declare provider + use canonical ref + autorouting works'. Specifically:\n\n- ddx-8610020e (RouteRequest.Provider field) — foundation\n- ddx-0486e601 (fuzzy normalization + per-provider) — lets cheap-tier model refs resolve to provider-native IDs\n- ddx-2f5a2284 (catalog/Profile discovery integration) — lets Profile tier overrides retarget to provider-native IDs\n- ddx-3c5ba7cc (tier escalation --provider affinity) — the lower half of this epic's 'escalate on failure' semantics\n- ddx-4817edfd (capability gating) — prevents routing to a model that can't serve the bead\n\nWith those in place, the profile-level policy here is mostly CONFIGURATION + a small amount of scoring-layer logic.\n\n## Scope\n\n### Config schema additions\n\n.ddx/config.yaml agent.routing extends with an explicit per-profile ladder:\n\n    agent:\n      routing:\n        profile_ladders:\n          default: [cheap, standard, smart]      # local first, escalate\n          cheap:   [cheap]                        # local only\n          fast:    [fast, smart]                  # cloud fast, escalate to smart\n          smart:   [smart]                        # cloud high-quality, no escalation\n        model_overrides:\n          cheap:    qwen/qwen3.6\n          fast:     kimi/k2.5\n          standard: codex/gpt-5.4                # new: subscription cloud, mid-quality\n          smart:    minimax/minimax-m2.7\n\nCurrent profile_priority field (a flat list) becomes a legacy alias for profile_ladders.default with a deprecation warning.\n\n### Tier-escalation policy (routing.go)\n\nFor a given profile P, iterate profile_ladders[P]:\n- Evaluate model_overrides[tier] resolves on a viable candidate (with --provider affinity from ddx-3c5ba7cc).\n- If yes, try it; on failure (execution_failed / land_conflict / post_run_check_failed), move to next tier.\n- Record tier decision + escalation chain in routing event on the bead so telemetry reflects where work actually landed.\n\n### User-facing CLI\n\n- ddx work --profile default — the new primary invocation. Most users run this.\n- ddx work --profile cheap / fast / smart — opt-in deviations.\n- --max-tier continues to cap the ladder (explicit user override beats config).\n\n### Telemetry + measurement\n\nEvery execution event records: requested_profile, requested_tier, resolved_tier, escalation_count, final_tier. Aggregated metrics (FEAT-016 / ddx-4b59b816) report per-profile throughput, per-tier cost, per-bead escalation rate.\n\n## Exit gate\n\n'ddx work --profile default --once --local' against a ready bead on a host with a healthy local provider AND healthy cloud fallback:\n- Claims the bead.\n- Tries cheap tier (local). If it succeeds, closes bead with tier=cheap recorded.\n- If cheap fails for any escalation-triggering reason, automatically retries at standard tier without operator intervention.\n- If standard fails, retries at smart.\n- Final outcome recorded faithfully (success at whatever tier landed, or exhaustion if all tiers failed).\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Dynamic profile selection ('auto-pick profile based on bead complexity'). Too much magic; explicit profile choice is fine.\n- Cost budgets / circuit breakers. FEAT-014 (token awareness) tracks this separately.\n- Per-bead profile overrides (bead-level 'requires-profile: smart'). Could be a future follow-up but out of scope here.\n\n## Metadata\n\nGoverning spec: FEAT-006 (agent service) + the autorouting epic ddx-2d974641. This bead is the PROFILE-layer policy that sits on top of the routing fixes from ddx-2d974641. Do not start until ddx-2d974641's Phase-1 children are closed.","events":[{"actor":"erik","body":"2026-04-18 dogfooding evidence: 2 omlx attempts at cheap tier, 2 no_changes.\n\nRun 1 — ddx-91bc770a (Checkpoint dirty parent trees before managed bead execution):\n- harness: agent / provider: vidar-omlx / model: Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit\n- duration: 1300s (21.7 min)\n- tokens: 254,413\n- outcome: task_no_changes — no rationale written\n- bead-class: substantial git-layer refactor\n\nRun 2 — ddx-e3493010 (Resolution Path developer guide):\n- harness: agent / provider: vidar-omlx / model: Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit\n- duration: 4150s (69.2 min)\n- tokens: 2,086,838 (2.09M)\n- outcome: task_no_changes — no rationale written\n- bead-class: documentation-only markdown authoring with code-walk citations\n\nFindings:\n\n1. v0.3.14 SSE filter is solid. 2M+ tokens streamed on run 2 with zero parse errors. Cheap-tier dispatch path no longer the blocker.\n\n2. Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit is not strong enough to reliably close real DDx beads. Both attempts burned significant wall-clock and tokens without committing. \"Free\" local compute has real opportunity cost when model thrashes for an hour without output.\n\n3. Without ladder escalation, operator has to manually fall back. This IS the motivating case for ddx-bbb65768: default profile should detect no_changes on first tier and escalate to standard (codex subscription) for a second try.\n\n4. AC #5 of this bead sets 50% close-at-cheap target over 10 representative beads. Current observed rate: 0% close-at-cheap over 2 representative beads. Epic delivery must LIFT this rate — either through better capability gating OR by making escalation cheap enough that failed cheap attempts don't dominate total cost.\n\n5. Qwen3-Coder-Next-MLX-4bit might do better on code-heavy beads; gemma-4-31B-it on docs. Worth follow-up experiment pinning different local models per bead-class after autorouting foundation lands.\n\nEvidence bundles: .ddx/executions/20260418T023901-ba3d8d59/ and 20260418T030326-*/.","created_at":"2026-04-18T04:14:00.46487895Z","kind":"dogfooding","source":"ddx bead evidence add","summary":"Qwen3.6 cheap-tier: 2 no_changes over 2 beads (21m + 69m, 2.34M tokens, 0 commits)"},{"actor":"ddx","body":"{\"resolved_provider\":\"codex\",\"fallback_chain\":[]}","created_at":"2026-04-21T16:58:36.212075005Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"provider=codex"},{"actor":"ddx","body":"{\"attempt_id\":\"20260421T163240-eb3cb31f\",\"harness\":\"codex\",\"input_tokens\":17441894,\"output_tokens\":42380,\"total_tokens\":17484274,\"cost_usd\":0,\"duration_ms\":1555475,\"exit_code\":0}","created_at":"2026-04-21T16:58:36.273033089Z","kind":"cost","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"tokens=17484274"}],"id":"ddx-bbb65768","issue_type":"epic","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:design","area:agent","area:routing","area:profiles","goal:throughput-per-dollar","wave:deferred","workstream:agent-upgrade"],"priority":2,"spec-id":"FEAT-006","status":"closed","title":"profile/automation: default = local-first with escalation; fast/smart = cloud-first","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:03:22.145529072Z"}

.agents/skills/.ddx/beads.jsonl:593:{"acceptance":"SD-015 defines a stable model-level attribution key for routing metrics so RoutingOutcome, QuotaSnapshot, and any derived burn/performance summaries can be associated with the resolved candidate model or canonical target; FEAT-014 stays consistent with that key; and the docs no longer imply that latency/cost/reliability from different models on the same surface are mixed into one routing signal","claimed-at":"2026-04-10T03:46:32Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"327749","closing_commit_sha":"0364b777bb79faf9eeef12a4d940b190d687c894","created_at":"2026-04-10T03:45:04.557331352Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-agent-routing-and-catalog-resolution.md:175\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: The new minimal-routing schema records `RoutingOutcome`, `QuotaSnapshot`, and `BurnSummary` only by `harness` and `surface`. Earlier in the same design, however, each `CandidatePlan` carries `CanonicalTarget`, `ConcreteModel`, `EstimatedCostUSD`, and `PerformanceMetrics` because routing decisions can differ materially by resolved model or exact pin. A single surface can host very different models with different latency, reliability, and cost characteristics. If DDx aggregates outcome and burn data only at the harness/surface level, runs for one model will contaminate routing signals for another, making model-specific scoring and exact-pin behavior incorrect.\nSuggested fix: Extend the minimal routing-metrics schema with a model-identifying dimension such as `canonical_target` or `concrete_model` (or explicitly define an equivalent normalization key), and update FEAT-014/SD-015 so performance, cost, and burn inputs remain attributable to the resolved model rather than only the surface.","id":"hx-4a12a619","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:medium","area:agent","area:docs"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-agent-routing-and-catalog-resolution.md","status":"closed","title":"correctness: keep routing metrics model-specific","updated_at":"2026-04-10T03:47:24.607997607Z"}

.agents/skills/.ddx/beads.jsonl:634:{"acceptance":"SD-016 states that cost_usd=-1 means unknown rather than known cost, direct cost aggregation excludes the sentinel value, and the cost_state / cost precedence rules align with FEAT-014 and FEAT-006 sentinel semantics","claimed-at":"2026-04-09T23:00:02Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"2091135","created_at":"2026-04-09T22:35:00.80036448Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-016-process-metrics.md:191\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: high\nDescription: SD-016 defines `cost_state=known` whenever a session row reports `cost_usd` and says bead lifecycle cost should use `session.cost_usd` whenever it is present. But FEAT-014 and FEAT-006 explicitly define `cost_usd=-1` as the sentinel for unknown cost, not a real price. Implementing SD-016 as written would treat unknown-cost sessions as known values, producing negative dollar totals and skipping the estimation/unknown fallback for a core FEAT-016 metric.\nSuggested fix: Update SD-016 so `cost_usd=-1` is mapped to `cost_state=unknown` and excluded from direct cost sums, with estimation or unknown fallback applied only to non-sentinel values.","id":"hx-72b9a368","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:high"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-016-process-metrics.md","status":"closed","title":"correctness: treat cost_usd=-1 as unknown in SD-016","updated_at":"2026-04-10T00:21:23.11757511Z"}

.claude/skills/.ddx/beads.jsonl:388:{"acceptance":"1. .ddx/config.yaml schema supports profile_ladders (per-profile ordered tier list) AND the legacy profile_priority is still readable with a deprecation warning.\n\n2. Four shipped profile ladders: default=[cheap,standard,smart], cheap=[cheap], fast=[fast,smart], smart=[smart]. Documented in FEAT-006.\n\n3. Tier escalation in routing.go / execute_bead_orchestrator.go iterates profile_ladders[P] on failure, respecting --provider affinity (ddx-3c5ba7cc) and capability gating (ddx-4817edfd). Escalation triggers: execution_failed, land_conflict, post_run_check_failed, structural_validation_failed. no_changes uses the existing cooldown path.\n\n4. Bead routing event records: requested_profile, requested_tier, resolved_tier, escalation_count, final_tier. A run that escalates cheap -\u003e standard -\u003e success at standard has an event with escalation_count=1, resolved_tier=standard, final_tier=standard.\n\n5. 'ddx work --profile default' drains the queue by trying local first and escalating only when needed. Measured outcome over a test run of 10 representative beads shows at least 50% closing at the cheap tier (target: most work lands locally; escalations are the exception).\n\n6. Regression tests cover each ladder path: success-at-cheap, escalate-cheap-to-standard, escalate-all-tiers-then-exhaustion, --max-tier caps the ladder, --profile cheap never escalates.\n\n7. FEAT-006 Profile Routing section updated with the ladder semantics and the intent table (default/cheap/fast/smart).","closing_commit_sha":"93f938c41c93be78fc199bd150f455add6575d24","created_at":"2026-04-18T02:51:49.512908486Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-bbb65768","depends_on_id":"ddx-2d974641","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-18T02:52:01Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-bbb65768","depends_on_id":"ddx-e7254128","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-20T13:58:09Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-bbb65768","depends_on_id":"ddx-7bc0c8d5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-20T14:36:21Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-bbb65768","depends_on_id":"ddx-ea7f041c","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-21T14:36:16Z"}],"description":"## Target UX\n\nThe user-facing profile vocabulary (cheap / default / fast / smart) should map to concrete routing policies that take full advantage of local models without blocking on their limits. Specifically:\n\n### default profile (the common case)\nTry local models first, escalate to cloud if:\n- local model's context window is too small for the bead prompt\n- local model lacks a capability the bead requires (tools, structured output, etc.)\n- local dispatch fails (provider unreachable, timeout, model-side error)\n- bead reopens due to post-run check failure (review-malfunction, land-conflict, etc.)\n\nEscalation target: next tier up in the ladder (standard cloud — e.g. codex gpt-5.4 within subscription). If THAT fails, escalate once more to smart cloud. Then stop and unclaim.\n\n### cheap profile\nLocal only. Never escalate. Intended for high-volume exploratory work where cost dominates and adequate-quality is sufficient. If the cheap tier can't serve the bead, the bead unclaims and waits.\n\n### fast profile\nCloud-first, skip local entirely. Intended for latency-critical or interactive work where local inference's 9s warmup is unacceptable. Starts at a fast cloud model (e.g. gemini-flash or haiku) and escalates to smart on failure.\n\n### smart profile\nCloud-high-quality from the start. Intended for bead-review, adversarial checks, or beads whose AC demands high-quality reasoning. No escalation — if smart fails, the work fails meaningfully.\n\n## Why this matters (dogfooding signal)\n\nAs of 2026-04-18, DDx's default is cheap = qwen/qwen3.6 local (vidar-omlx). With v0.3.14 shipping the SSE-comment-frame filter, the local path actually works end-to-end. The queue can drain mostly on local hardware for pennies per bead, escalating only when the bead genuinely needs cloud quality. That's the throughput-per-dollar goal (goal:throughput-per-dollar label).\n\n## Prereqs (already filed under ddx-2d974641 or sibling beads)\n\nThis epic is blocked on the autorouting epic delivering its exit-gate UX — 'declare provider + use canonical ref + autorouting works'. Specifically:\n\n- ddx-8610020e (RouteRequest.Provider field) — foundation\n- ddx-0486e601 (fuzzy normalization + per-provider) — lets cheap-tier model refs resolve to provider-native IDs\n- ddx-2f5a2284 (catalog/Profile discovery integration) — lets Profile tier overrides retarget to provider-native IDs\n- ddx-3c5ba7cc (tier escalation --provider affinity) — the lower half of this epic's 'escalate on failure' semantics\n- ddx-4817edfd (capability gating) — prevents routing to a model that can't serve the bead\n\nWith those in place, the profile-level policy here is mostly CONFIGURATION + a small amount of scoring-layer logic.\n\n## Scope\n\n### Config schema additions\n\n.ddx/config.yaml agent.routing extends with an explicit per-profile ladder:\n\n    agent:\n      routing:\n        profile_ladders:\n          default: [cheap, standard, smart]      # local first, escalate\n          cheap:   [cheap]                        # local only\n          fast:    [fast, smart]                  # cloud fast, escalate to smart\n          smart:   [smart]                        # cloud high-quality, no escalation\n        model_overrides:\n          cheap:    qwen/qwen3.6\n          fast:     kimi/k2.5\n          standard: codex/gpt-5.4                # new: subscription cloud, mid-quality\n          smart:    minimax/minimax-m2.7\n\nCurrent profile_priority field (a flat list) becomes a legacy alias for profile_ladders.default with a deprecation warning.\n\n### Tier-escalation policy (routing.go)\n\nFor a given profile P, iterate profile_ladders[P]:\n- Evaluate model_overrides[tier] resolves on a viable candidate (with --provider affinity from ddx-3c5ba7cc).\n- If yes, try it; on failure (execution_failed / land_conflict / post_run_check_failed), move to next tier.\n- Record tier decision + escalation chain in routing event on the bead so telemetry reflects where work actually landed.\n\n### User-facing CLI\n\n- ddx work --profile default — the new primary invocation. Most users run this.\n- ddx work --profile cheap / fast / smart — opt-in deviations.\n- --max-tier continues to cap the ladder (explicit user override beats config).\n\n### Telemetry + measurement\n\nEvery execution event records: requested_profile, requested_tier, resolved_tier, escalation_count, final_tier. Aggregated metrics (FEAT-016 / ddx-4b59b816) report per-profile throughput, per-tier cost, per-bead escalation rate.\n\n## Exit gate\n\n'ddx work --profile default --once --local' against a ready bead on a host with a healthy local provider AND healthy cloud fallback:\n- Claims the bead.\n- Tries cheap tier (local). If it succeeds, closes bead with tier=cheap recorded.\n- If cheap fails for any escalation-triggering reason, automatically retries at standard tier without operator intervention.\n- If standard fails, retries at smart.\n- Final outcome recorded faithfully (success at whatever tier landed, or exhaustion if all tiers failed).\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Dynamic profile selection ('auto-pick profile based on bead complexity'). Too much magic; explicit profile choice is fine.\n- Cost budgets / circuit breakers. FEAT-014 (token awareness) tracks this separately.\n- Per-bead profile overrides (bead-level 'requires-profile: smart'). Could be a future follow-up but out of scope here.\n\n## Metadata\n\nGoverning spec: FEAT-006 (agent service) + the autorouting epic ddx-2d974641. This bead is the PROFILE-layer policy that sits on top of the routing fixes from ddx-2d974641. Do not start until ddx-2d974641's Phase-1 children are closed.","events":[{"actor":"erik","body":"2026-04-18 dogfooding evidence: 2 omlx attempts at cheap tier, 2 no_changes.\n\nRun 1 — ddx-91bc770a (Checkpoint dirty parent trees before managed bead execution):\n- harness: agent / provider: vidar-omlx / model: Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit\n- duration: 1300s (21.7 min)\n- tokens: 254,413\n- outcome: task_no_changes — no rationale written\n- bead-class: substantial git-layer refactor\n\nRun 2 — ddx-e3493010 (Resolution Path developer guide):\n- harness: agent / provider: vidar-omlx / model: Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit\n- duration: 4150s (69.2 min)\n- tokens: 2,086,838 (2.09M)\n- outcome: task_no_changes — no rationale written\n- bead-class: documentation-only markdown authoring with code-walk citations\n\nFindings:\n\n1. v0.3.14 SSE filter is solid. 2M+ tokens streamed on run 2 with zero parse errors. Cheap-tier dispatch path no longer the blocker.\n\n2. Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit is not strong enough to reliably close real DDx beads. Both attempts burned significant wall-clock and tokens without committing. \"Free\" local compute has real opportunity cost when model thrashes for an hour without output.\n\n3. Without ladder escalation, operator has to manually fall back. This IS the motivating case for ddx-bbb65768: default profile should detect no_changes on first tier and escalate to standard (codex subscription) for a second try.\n\n4. AC #5 of this bead sets 50% close-at-cheap target over 10 representative beads. Current observed rate: 0% close-at-cheap over 2 representative beads. Epic delivery must LIFT this rate — either through better capability gating OR by making escalation cheap enough that failed cheap attempts don't dominate total cost.\n\n5. Qwen3-Coder-Next-MLX-4bit might do better on code-heavy beads; gemma-4-31B-it on docs. Worth follow-up experiment pinning different local models per bead-class after autorouting foundation lands.\n\nEvidence bundles: .ddx/executions/20260418T023901-ba3d8d59/ and 20260418T030326-*/.","created_at":"2026-04-18T04:14:00.46487895Z","kind":"dogfooding","source":"ddx bead evidence add","summary":"Qwen3.6 cheap-tier: 2 no_changes over 2 beads (21m + 69m, 2.34M tokens, 0 commits)"},{"actor":"ddx","body":"{\"resolved_provider\":\"codex\",\"fallback_chain\":[]}","created_at":"2026-04-21T16:58:36.212075005Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"provider=codex"},{"actor":"ddx","body":"{\"attempt_id\":\"20260421T163240-eb3cb31f\",\"harness\":\"codex\",\"input_tokens\":17441894,\"output_tokens\":42380,\"total_tokens\":17484274,\"cost_usd\":0,\"duration_ms\":1555475,\"exit_code\":0}","created_at":"2026-04-21T16:58:36.273033089Z","kind":"cost","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"tokens=17484274"}],"id":"ddx-bbb65768","issue_type":"epic","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:design","area:agent","area:routing","area:profiles","goal:throughput-per-dollar","wave:deferred","workstream:agent-upgrade"],"priority":2,"spec-id":"FEAT-006","status":"closed","title":"profile/automation: default = local-first with escalation; fast/smart = cloud-first","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:03:22.145529072Z"}

Earlier in the same design, however, each `CandidatePlan` carries `CanonicalTarget`, `ConcreteModel`, `EstimatedCostUSD`, and `PerformanceMetrics` because routing decisions can differ materially by resolved model or exact pin. A single surface can host very different models with different latency, reliability, and cost characteristics. If DDx aggregates outcome and burn data only at the harness/surface level, runs for one model will contaminate routing signals for another, making model-specific scoring and exact-pin behavior incorrect.\nSuggested fix: Extend the minimal routing-metrics schema with a model-identifying dimension such as `canonical_target` or `concrete_model` (or explicitly define an equivalent normalization key), and update FEAT-014/SD-015 so performance, cost, and burn inputs remain attributable to the resolved model rather than only the surface.","id":"hx-4a12a619","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:medium","area:agent","area:docs"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-agent-routing-and-catalog-resolution.md","status":"closed","title":"correctness: keep routing metrics model-specific","updated_at":"2026-04-10T03:47:24.607997607Z"}

.claude/skills/.ddx/beads.jsonl:634:{"acceptance":"SD-016 states that cost_usd=-1 means unknown rather than known cost, direct cost aggregation excludes the sentinel value, and the cost_state / cost precedence rules align with FEAT-014 and FEAT-006 sentinel semantics","claimed-at":"2026-04-09T23:00:02Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"2091135","created_at":"2026-04-09T22:35:00.80036448Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-016-process-metrics.md:191\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: high\nDescription: SD-016 defines `cost_state=known` whenever a session row reports `cost_usd` and says bead lifecycle cost should use `session.cost_usd` whenever it is present. But FEAT-014 and FEAT-006 explicitly define `cost_usd=-1` as the sentinel for unknown cost, not a real price. Implementing SD-016 as written would treat unknown-cost sessions as known values, producing negative dollar totals and skipping the estimation/unknown fallback for a core FEAT-016 metric.\nSuggested fix: Update SD-016 so `cost_usd=-1` is mapped to `cost_state=unknown` and excluded from direct cost sums, with estimation or unknown fallback applied only to non-sentinel values.","id":"hx-72b9a368","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:high"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-016-process-metrics.md","status":"closed","title":"correctness: treat cost_usd=-1 as unknown in SD-016","updated_at":"2026-04-10T00:21:23.11757511Z"}

.agents/skills/.ddx/executions/20260411T165740-77adba47/manifest.json:12:    "description": "\u003ccontext-digest\u003e\nReview area: host+user ddx-server planning stack after the server model evolved beyond the earlier multi-project topology draft. Evidence covers FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, TP-002, the fresh-eyes server review, and the updated requirements for a per-user host daemon, localhost HTTP UI, SQLite-backed runtime/index state, embedded-agent progress visibility, provider dashboarding, replay-backed fixtures, and Playwright coverage for every new UI surface.\n\u003c/context-digest\u003e\nUpdate the governing server planning docs so they describe the current intended host+user ddx-server model before follow-on server design and implementation beads continue.\n\n## Goals\n- Reconcile FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, and TP-002 around a single host+user ddx-server instance with explicit project registry, worker management boundaries, and queue semantics\n- Define the split between git-backed project truth, tracked execution evidence, and host-local runtime/index state backed by an embedded database\n- Define the current transport/security model: localhost unauthenticated HTTP for UI/API now, explicit future transport/auth evolution later\n- Add the embedded-agent progress, provider dashboard, replay-backed fixture, and Playwright coverage requirements to the governed server plan\n- Make the scheduling boundary explicit: server may allocate workers across projects, but bead ordering remains owned by each project queue\n\n## Boundaries\n- Keep Axon and multi-host service-backed topology as later-stage work\n- Do not reopen the already-shipped single-project execute-bead loop contract except where the new server plan must reference it",

Evidence covers FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, TP-002, the fresh-eyes server review, and the updated requirements for a per-user host daemon, localhost HTTP UI, SQLite-backed runtime/index state, embedded-agent progress visibility, provider dashboarding, replay-backed fixtures, and Playwright coverage for every new UI surface.\n\u003c/context-digest\u003e\nUpdate the governing server planning docs so they describe the current intended host+user ddx-server model before follow-on server design and implementation beads continue.\n\n## Goals\n- Reconcile FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, and TP-002 around a single host+user ddx-server instance with explicit project registry, worker management boundaries, and queue semantics\n- Define the split between git-backed project truth, tracked execution evidence, and host-local runtime/index state backed by an embedded database\n- Define the current transport/security model: localhost unauthenticated HTTP for UI/API now, explicit future transport/auth evolution later\n- Add the embedded-agent progress, provider dashboard, replay-backed fixture, and Playwright coverage requirements to the governed server plan\n- Make the scheduling boundary explicit: server may allocate workers across projects, but bead ordering remains owned by each project queue\n\n## Boundaries\n- Keep Axon and multi-host service-backed topology as later-stage work\n- Do not reopen the already-shipped single-project execute-bead loop contract except where the new server plan must reference it",

.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:628:{"acceptance":"1. .ddx/config.yaml schema supports profile_ladders (per-profile ordered tier list) AND the legacy profile_priority is still readable with a deprecation warning.\n\n2. Four shipped profile ladders: default=[cheap,standard,smart], cheap=[cheap], fast=[fast,smart], smart=[smart]. Documented in FEAT-006.\n\n3. Tier escalation in routing.go / execute_bead_orchestrator.go iterates profile_ladders[P] on failure, respecting --provider affinity (ddx-3c5ba7cc) and capability gating (ddx-4817edfd). Escalation triggers: execution_failed, land_conflict, post_run_check_failed, structural_validation_failed. no_changes uses the existing cooldown path.\n\n4. Bead routing event records: requested_profile, requested_tier, resolved_tier, escalation_count, final_tier. A run that escalates cheap -\u003e standard -\u003e success at standard has an event with escalation_count=1, resolved_tier=standard, final_tier=standard.\n\n5. 'ddx work --profile default' drains the queue by trying local first and escalating only when needed. Measured outcome over a test run of 10 representative beads shows at least 50% closing at the cheap tier (target: most work lands locally; escalations are the exception).\n\n6. Regression tests cover each ladder path: success-at-cheap, escalate-cheap-to-standard, escalate-all-tiers-then-exhaustion, --max-tier caps the ladder, --profile cheap never escalates.\n\n7. FEAT-006 Profile Routing section updated with the ladder semantics and the intent table (default/cheap/fast/smart).","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","closing_commit_sha":"93f938c41c93be78fc199bd150f455add6575d24","created_at":"2026-04-18T02:51:49.512908486Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-bbb65768","depends_on_id":"ddx-2d974641","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-18T02:52:01Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-bbb65768","depends_on_id":"ddx-e7254128","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-20T13:58:09Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-bbb65768","depends_on_id":"ddx-7bc0c8d5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-20T14:36:21Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-bbb65768","depends_on_id":"ddx-ea7f041c","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-21T14:36:16Z"}],"description":"## Target UX\n\nThe user-facing profile vocabulary (cheap / default / fast / smart) should map to concrete routing policies that take full advantage of local models without blocking on their limits. Specifically:\n\n### default profile (the common case)\nTry local models first, escalate to cloud if:\n- local model's context window is too small for the bead prompt\n- local model lacks a capability the bead requires (tools, structured output, etc.)\n- local dispatch fails (provider unreachable, timeout, model-side error)\n- bead reopens due to post-run check failure (review-malfunction, land-conflict, etc.)\n\nEscalation target: next tier up in the ladder (standard cloud — e.g. codex gpt-5.4 within subscription). If THAT fails, escalate once more to smart cloud. Then stop and unclaim.\n\n### cheap profile\nLocal only. Never escalate. Intended for high-volume exploratory work where cost dominates and adequate-quality is sufficient. If the cheap tier can't serve the bead, the bead unclaims and waits.\n\n### fast profile\nCloud-first, skip local entirely. Intended for latency-critical or interactive work where local inference's 9s warmup is unacceptable. Starts at a fast cloud model (e.g. gemini-flash or haiku) and escalates to smart on failure.\n\n### smart profile\nCloud-high-quality from the start. Intended for bead-review, adversarial checks, or beads whose AC demands high-quality reasoning. No escalation — if smart fails, the work fails meaningfully.\n\n## Why this matters (dogfooding signal)\n\nAs of 2026-04-18, DDx's default is cheap = qwen/qwen3.6 local (vidar-omlx). With v0.3.14 shipping the SSE-comment-frame filter, the local path actually works end-to-end. The queue can drain mostly on local hardware for pennies per bead, escalating only when the bead genuinely needs cloud quality. That's the throughput-per-dollar goal (goal:throughput-per-dollar label).\n\n## Prereqs (already filed under ddx-2d974641 or sibling beads)\n\nThis epic is blocked on the autorouting epic delivering its exit-gate UX — 'declare provider + use canonical ref + autorouting works'. Specifically:\n\n- ddx-8610020e (RouteRequest.Provider field) — foundation\n- ddx-0486e601 (fuzzy normalization + per-provider) — lets cheap-tier model refs resolve to provider-native IDs\n- ddx-2f5a2284 (catalog/Profile discovery integration) — lets Profile tier overrides retarget to provider-native IDs\n- ddx-3c5ba7cc (tier escalation --provider affinity) — the lower half of this epic's 'escalate on failure' semantics\n- ddx-4817edfd (capability gating) — prevents routing to a model that can't serve the bead\n\nWith those in place, the profile-level policy here is mostly CONFIGURATION + a small amount of scoring-layer logic.\n\n## Scope\n\n### Config schema additions\n\n.ddx/config.yaml agent.routing extends with an explicit per-profile ladder:\n\n    agent:\n      routing:\n        profile_ladders:\n          default: [cheap, standard, smart]      # local first, escalate\n          cheap:   [cheap]                        # local only\n          fast:    [fast, smart]                  # cloud fast, escalate to smart\n          smart:   [smart]                        # cloud high-quality, no escalation\n        model_overrides:\n          cheap:    qwen/qwen3.6\n          fast:     kimi/k2.5\n          standard: codex/gpt-5.4                # new: subscription cloud, mid-quality\n          smart:    minimax/minimax-m2.7\n\nCurrent profile_priority field (a flat list) becomes a legacy alias for profile_ladders.default with a deprecation warning.\n\n### Tier-escalation policy (routing.go)\n\nFor a given profile P, iterate profile_ladders[P]:\n- Evaluate model_overrides[tier] resolves on a viable candidate (with --provider affinity from ddx-3c5ba7cc).\n- If yes, try it; on failure (execution_failed / land_conflict / post_run_check_failed), move to next tier.\n- Record tier decision + escalation chain in routing event on the bead so telemetry reflects where work actually landed.\n\n### User-facing CLI\n\n- ddx work --profile default — the new primary invocation. Most users run this.\n- ddx work --profile cheap / fast / smart — opt-in deviations.\n- --max-tier continues to cap the ladder (explicit user override beats config).\n\n### Telemetry + measurement\n\nEvery execution event records: requested_profile, requested_tier, resolved_tier, escalation_count, final_tier. Aggregated metrics (FEAT-016 / ddx-4b59b816) report per-profile throughput, per-tier cost, per-bead escalation rate.\n\n## Exit gate\n\n'ddx work --profile default --once --local' against a ready bead on a host with a healthy local provider AND healthy cloud fallback:\n- Claims the bead.\n- Tries cheap tier (local). If it succeeds, closes bead with tier=cheap recorded.\n- If cheap fails for any escalation-triggering reason, automatically retries at standard tier without operator intervention.\n- If standard fails, retries at smart.\n- Final outcome recorded faithfully (success at whatever tier landed, or exhaustion if all tiers failed).\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Dynamic profile selection ('auto-pick profile based on bead complexity'). Too much magic; explicit profile choice is fine.\n- Cost budgets / circuit breakers. FEAT-014 (token awareness) tracks this separately.\n- Per-bead profile overrides (bead-level 'requires-profile: smart'). Could be a future follow-up but out of scope here.\n\n## Metadata\n\nGoverning spec: FEAT-006 (agent service) + the autorouting epic ddx-2d974641. This bead is the PROFILE-layer policy that sits on top of the routing fixes from ddx-2d974641. Do not start until ddx-2d974641's Phase-1 children are closed.","events_attachment":"ddx-bbb65768/events.jsonl","id":"ddx-bbb65768","issue_type":"epic","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:design","area:agent","area:routing","area:profiles","goal:throughput-per-dollar","wave:deferred","workstream:agent-upgrade"],"priority":2,"spec-id":"FEAT-006","status":"closed","title":"profile/automation: default = local-first with escalation; fast/smart = cloud-first","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:03:22.145529072Z"}

.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:934:{"acceptance":"SD-015 defines a stable model-level attribution key for routing metrics so RoutingOutcome, QuotaSnapshot, and any derived burn/performance summaries can be associated with the resolved candidate model or canonical target; FEAT-014 stays consistent with that key; and the docs no longer imply that latency/cost/reliability from different models on the same surface are mixed into one routing signal","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-10T03:46:32Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"327749","closing_commit_sha":"0364b777bb79faf9eeef12a4d940b190d687c894","created_at":"2026-04-10T03:45:04.557331352Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-agent-routing-and-catalog-resolution.md:175\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: The new minimal-routing schema records `RoutingOutcome`, `QuotaSnapshot`, and `BurnSummary` only by `harness` and `surface`. Earlier in the same design, however, each `CandidatePlan` carries `CanonicalTarget`, `ConcreteModel`, `EstimatedCostUSD`, and `PerformanceMetrics` because routing decisions can differ materially by resolved model or exact pin. A single surface can host very different models with different latency, reliability, and cost characteristics. If DDx aggregates outcome and burn data only at the harness/surface level, runs for one model will contaminate routing signals for another, making model-specific scoring and exact-pin behavior incorrect.\nSuggested fix: Extend the minimal routing-metrics schema with a model-identifying dimension such as `canonical_target` or `concrete_model` (or explicitly define an equivalent normalization key), and update FEAT-014/SD-015 so performance, cost, and burn inputs remain attributable to the resolved model rather than only the surface.","id":"hx-4a12a619","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:medium","area:agent","area:docs"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-agent-routing-and-catalog-resolution.md","status":"closed","title":"correctness: keep routing metrics model-specific","updated_at":"2026-04-10T03:47:24.607997607Z"}

.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:975:{"acceptance":"SD-016 states that cost_usd=-1 means unknown rather than known cost, direct cost aggregation excludes the sentinel value, and the cost_state / cost precedence rules align with FEAT-014 and FEAT-006 sentinel semantics","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-09T23:00:02Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"2091135","created_at":"2026-04-09T22:35:00.80036448Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-016-process-metrics.md:191\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: high\nDescription: SD-016 defines `cost_state=known` whenever a session row reports `cost_usd` and says bead lifecycle cost should use `session.cost_usd` whenever it is present. But FEAT-014 and FEAT-006 explicitly define `cost_usd=-1` as the sentinel for unknown cost, not a real price. Implementing SD-016 as written would treat unknown-cost sessions as known values, producing negative dollar totals and skipping the estimation/unknown fallback for a core FEAT-016 metric.\nSuggested fix: Update SD-016 so `cost_usd=-1` is mapped to `cost_state=unknown` and excluded from direct cost sums, with estimation or unknown fallback applied only to non-sentinel values.","id":"hx-72b9a368","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:high"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-016-process-metrics.md","status":"closed","title":"correctness: treat cost_usd=-1 as unknown in SD-016","updated_at":"2026-04-10T00:21:23.11757511Z"}

.agents/skills/.ddx/executions/20260421T163240-eb3cb31f/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Target UX\n\nThe user-facing profile vocabulary (cheap / default / fast / smart) should map to concrete routing policies that take full advantage of local models without blocking on their limits. Specifically:\n\n### default profile (the common case)\nTry local models first, escalate to cloud if:\n- local model's context window is too small for the bead prompt\n- local model lacks a capability the bead requires (tools, structured output, etc.)\n- local dispatch fails (provider unreachable, timeout, model-side error)\n- bead reopens due to post-run check failure (review-malfunction, land-conflict, etc.)\n\nEscalation target: next tier up in the ladder (standard cloud — e.g. codex gpt-5.4 within subscription). If THAT fails, escalate once more to smart cloud. Then stop and unclaim.\n\n### cheap profile\nLocal only. Never escalate. Intended for high-volume exploratory work where cost dominates and adequate-quality is sufficient. If the cheap tier can't serve the bead, the bead unclaims and waits.\n\n### fast profile\nCloud-first, skip local entirely. Intended for latency-critical or interactive work where local inference's 9s warmup is unacceptable. Starts at a fast cloud model (e.g. gemini-flash or haiku) and escalates to smart on failure.\n\n### smart profile\nCloud-high-quality from the start. Intended for bead-review, adversarial checks, or beads whose AC demands high-quality reasoning. No escalation — if smart fails, the work fails meaningfully.\n\n## Why this matters (dogfooding signal)\n\nAs of 2026-04-18, DDx's default is cheap = qwen/qwen3.6 local (vidar-omlx). With v0.3.14 shipping the SSE-comment-frame filter, the local path actually works end-to-end. The queue can drain mostly on local hardware for pennies per bead, escalating only when the bead genuinely needs cloud quality. That's the throughput-per-dollar goal (goal:throughput-per-dollar label).\n\n## Prereqs (already filed under ddx-2d974641 or sibling beads)\n\nThis epic is blocked on the autorouting epic delivering its exit-gate UX — 'declare provider + use canonical ref + autorouting works'. Specifically:\n\n- ddx-8610020e (RouteRequest.Provider field) — foundation\n- ddx-0486e601 (fuzzy normalization + per-provider) — lets cheap-tier model refs resolve to provider-native IDs\n- ddx-2f5a2284 (catalog/Profile discovery integration) — lets Profile tier overrides retarget to provider-native IDs\n- ddx-3c5ba7cc (tier escalation --provider affinity) — the lower half of this epic's 'escalate on failure' semantics\n- ddx-4817edfd (capability gating) — prevents routing to a model that can't serve the bead\n\nWith those in place, the profile-level policy here is mostly CONFIGURATION + a small amount of scoring-layer logic.\n\n## Scope\n\n### Config schema additions\n\n.ddx/config.yaml agent.routing extends with an explicit per-profile ladder:\n\n    agent:\n      routing:\n        profile_ladders:\n          default: [cheap, standard, smart]      # local first, escalate\n          cheap:   [cheap]                        # local only\n          fast:    [fast, smart]                  # cloud fast, escalate to smart\n          smart:   [smart]                        # cloud high-quality, no escalation\n        model_overrides:\n          cheap:    qwen/qwen3.6\n          fast:     kimi/k2.5\n          standard: codex/gpt-5.4                # new: subscription cloud, mid-quality\n          smart:    minimax/minimax-m2.7\n\nCurrent profile_priority field (a flat list) becomes a legacy alias for profile_ladders.default with a deprecation warning.\n\n### Tier-escalation policy (routing.go)\n\nFor a given profile P, iterate profile_ladders[P]:\n- Evaluate model_overrides[tier] resolves on a viable candidate (with --provider affinity from ddx-3c5ba7cc).\n- If yes, try it; on failure (execution_failed / land_conflict / post_run_check_failed), move to next tier.\n- Record tier decision + escalation chain in routing event on the bead so telemetry reflects where work actually landed.\n\n### User-facing CLI\n\n- ddx work --profile default — the new primary invocation. Most users run this.\n- ddx work --profile cheap / fast / smart — opt-in deviations.\n- --max-tier continues to cap the ladder (explicit user override beats config).\n\n### Telemetry + measurement\n\nEvery execution event records: requested_profile, requested_tier, resolved_tier, escalation_count, final_tier. Aggregated metrics (FEAT-016 / ddx-4b59b816) report per-profile throughput, per-tier cost, per-bead escalation rate.\n\n## Exit gate\n\n'ddx work --profile default --once --local' against a ready bead on a host with a healthy local provider AND healthy cloud fallback:\n- Claims the bead.\n- Tries cheap tier (local). If it succeeds, closes bead with tier=cheap recorded.\n- If cheap fails for any escalation-triggering reason, automatically retries at standard tier without operator intervention.\n- If standard fails, retries at smart.\n- Final outcome recorded faithfully (success at whatever tier landed, or exhaustion if all tiers failed).\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Dynamic profile selection ('auto-pick profile based on bead complexity'). Too much magic; explicit profile choice is fine.\n- Cost budgets / circuit breakers. FEAT-014 (token awareness) tracks this separately.\n- Per-bead profile overrides (bead-level 'requires-profile: smart'). Could be a future follow-up but out of scope here.\n\n## Metadata\n\nGoverning spec: FEAT-006 (agent service) + the autorouting epic ddx-2d974641. This bead is the PROFILE-layer policy that sits on top of the routing fixes from ddx-2d974641. Do not start until ddx-2d974641's Phase-1 children are closed.",
.agents/skills/.ddx/executions/20260421T163240-eb3cb31f/prompt.md:5:## Target UX

The user-facing profile vocabulary (cheap / default / fast / smart) should map to concrete routing policies that take full advantage of local models without blocking on their limits. Specifically:

### default profile (the common case)
Try local models first, escalate to cloud if:
- local model's context window is too small for the bead prompt
- local model lacks a capability the bead requires (tools, structured output, etc.)
- local dispatch fails (provider unreachable, timeout, model-side error)
- bead reopens due to post-run check failure (review-malfunction, land-conflict, etc.)

Escalation target: next tier up in the ladder (standard cloud — e.g. codex gpt-5.4 within subscription). If THAT fails, escalate once more to smart cloud. Then stop and unclaim.

### cheap profile
Local only. Never escalate. Intended for high-volume exploratory work where cost dominates and adequate-quality is sufficient. If the cheap tier can't serve the bead, the bead unclaims and waits.

### fast profile
Cloud-first, skip local entirely. Intended for latency-critical or interactive work where local inference's 9s warmup is unacceptable. Starts at a fast cloud model (e.g. gemini-flash or haiku) and escalates to smart on failure.

### smart profile
Cloud-high-quality from the start. Intended for bead-review, adversarial checks, or beads whose AC demands high-quality reasoning. No escalation — if smart fails, the work fails meaningfully.

## Why this matters (dogfooding signal)

As of 2026-04-18, DDx's default is cheap = qwen/qwen3.6 local (vidar-omlx). With v0.3.14 shipping the SSE-comment-frame filter, the local path actually works end-to-end. The queue can drain mostly on local hardware for pennies per bead, escalating only when the bead genuinely needs cloud quality. That's the throughput-per-dollar goal (goal:throughput-per-dollar label).

## Prereqs (already filed under ddx-2d974641 or sibling beads)

This epic is blocked on the autorouting epic delivering its exit-gate UX — 'declare provider + use canonical ref + autorouting works'. Specifically:

- ddx-8610020e (RouteRequest.Provider field) — foundation
- ddx-0486e601 (fuzzy normalization + per-provider) — lets cheap-tier model refs resolve to provider-native IDs
- ddx-2f5a2284 (catalog/Profile discovery integration) — lets Profile tier overrides retarget to provider-native IDs
- ddx-3c5ba7cc (tier escalation --provider affinity) — the lower half of this epic's 'escalate on failure' semantics
- ddx-4817edfd (capability gating) — prevents routing to a model that can't serve the bead

With those in place, the profile-level policy here is mostly CONFIGURATION + a small amount of scoring-layer logic.

## Scope

### Config schema additions

.ddx/config.yaml agent.routing extends with an explicit per-profile ladder:

    agent:
      routing:
        profile_ladders:
          default: [cheap, standard, smart]      # local first, escalate
          cheap:   [cheap]                        # local only
          fast:    [fast, smart]                  # cloud fast, escalate to smart
          smart:   [smart]                        # cloud high-quality, no escalation
        model_overrides:
          cheap:    qwen/qwen3.6
          fast:     kimi/k2.5
          standard: codex/gpt-5.4                # new: subscription cloud, mid-quality
          smart:    minimax/minimax-m2.7

Current profile_priority field (a flat list) becomes a legacy alias for profile_ladders.default with a deprecation warning.

### Tier-escalation policy (routing.go)

For a given profile P, iterate profile_ladders[P]:
- Evaluate model_overrides[tier] resolves on a viable candidate (with --provider affinity from ddx-3c5ba7cc).
- If yes, try it; on failure (execution_failed / land_conflict / post_run_check_failed), move to next tier.
- Record tier decision + escalation chain in routing event on the bead so telemetry reflects where work actually landed.

### User-facing CLI

- ddx work --profile default — the new primary invocation. Most users run this.
- ddx work --profile cheap / fast / smart — opt-in deviations.
- --max-tier continues to cap the ladder (explicit user override beats config).

### Telemetry + measurement

Every execution event records: requested_profile, requested_tier, resolved_tier, escalation_count, final_tier. Aggregated metrics (FEAT-016 / ddx-4b59b816) report per-profile throughput, per-tier cost, per-bead escalation rate.

## Exit gate

'ddx work --profile default --once --local' against a ready bead on a host with a healthy local provider AND healthy cloud fallback:
- Claims the bead.
- Tries cheap tier (local). If it succeeds, closes bead with tier=cheap recorded.
- If cheap fails for any escalation-triggering reason, automatically retries at standard tier without operator intervention.
- If standard fails, retries at smart.
- Final outcome recorded faithfully (success at whatever tier landed, or exhaustion if all tiers failed).

## Out of scope

- Dynamic profile selection ('auto-pick profile based on bead complexity'). Too much magic; explicit profile choice is fine.
- Cost budgets / circuit breakers. FEAT-014 (token awareness) tracks this separately.
- Per-bead profile overrides (bead-level 'requires-profile: smart'). Could be a future follow-up but out of scope here.

## Metadata

Governing spec: FEAT-006 (agent service) + the autorouting epic ddx-2d974641. This bead is the PROFILE-layer policy that sits on top of the routing fixes from ddx-2d974641. Do not start until ddx-2d974641's Phase-1 children are closed.

.claude/skills/.ddx/executions/20260421T163240-eb3cb31f/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Target UX\n\nThe user-facing profile vocabulary (cheap / default / fast / smart) should map to concrete routing policies that take full advantage of local models without blocking on their limits. Specifically:\n\n### default profile (the common case)\nTry local models first, escalate to cloud if:\n- local model's context window is too small for the bead prompt\n- local model lacks a capability the bead requires (tools, structured output, etc.)\n- local dispatch fails (provider unreachable, timeout, model-side error)\n- bead reopens due to post-run check failure (review-malfunction, land-conflict, etc.)\n\nEscalation target: next tier up in the ladder (standard cloud — e.g. codex gpt-5.4 within subscription). If THAT fails, escalate once more to smart cloud. Then stop and unclaim.\n\n### cheap profile\nLocal only. Never escalate. Intended for high-volume exploratory work where cost dominates and adequate-quality is sufficient. If the cheap tier can't serve the bead, the bead unclaims and waits.\n\n### fast profile\nCloud-first, skip local entirely. Intended for latency-critical or interactive work where local inference's 9s warmup is unacceptable. Starts at a fast cloud model (e.g. gemini-flash or haiku) and escalates to smart on failure.\n\n### smart profile\nCloud-high-quality from the start. Intended for bead-review, adversarial checks, or beads whose AC demands high-quality reasoning. No escalation — if smart fails, the work fails meaningfully.\n\n## Why this matters (dogfooding signal)\n\nAs of 2026-04-18, DDx's default is cheap = qwen/qwen3.6 local (vidar-omlx). With v0.3.14 shipping the SSE-comment-frame filter, the local path actually works end-to-end. The queue can drain mostly on local hardware for pennies per bead, escalating only when the bead genuinely needs cloud quality. That's the throughput-per-dollar goal (goal:throughput-per-dollar label).\n\n## Prereqs (already filed under ddx-2d974641 or sibling beads)\n\nThis epic is blocked on the autorouting epic delivering its exit-gate UX — 'declare provider + use canonical ref + autorouting works'. Specifically:\n\n- ddx-8610020e (RouteRequest.Provider field) — foundation\n- ddx-0486e601 (fuzzy normalization + per-provider) — lets cheap-tier model refs resolve to provider-native IDs\n- ddx-2f5a2284 (catalog/Profile discovery integration) — lets Profile tier overrides retarget to provider-native IDs\n- ddx-3c5ba7cc (tier escalation --provider affinity) — the lower half of this epic's 'escalate on failure' semantics\n- ddx-4817edfd (capability gating) — prevents routing to a model that can't serve the bead\n\nWith those in place, the profile-level policy here is mostly CONFIGURATION + a small amount of scoring-layer logic.\n\n## Scope\n\n### Config schema additions\n\n.ddx/config.yaml agent.routing extends with an explicit per-profile ladder:\n\n    agent:\n      routing:\n        profile_ladders:\n          default: [cheap, standard, smart]      # local first, escalate\n          cheap:   [cheap]                        # local only\n          fast:    [fast, smart]                  # cloud fast, escalate to smart\n          smart:   [smart]                        # cloud high-quality, no escalation\n        model_overrides:\n          cheap:    qwen/qwen3.6\n          fast:     kimi/k2.5\n          standard: codex/gpt-5.4                # new: subscription cloud, mid-quality\n          smart:    minimax/minimax-m2.7\n\nCurrent profile_priority field (a flat list) becomes a legacy alias for profile_ladders.default with a deprecation warning.\n\n### Tier-escalation policy (routing.go)\n\nFor a given profile P, iterate profile_ladders[P]:\n- Evaluate model_overrides[tier] resolves on a viable candidate (with --provider affinity from ddx-3c5ba7cc).\n- If yes, try it; on failure (execution_failed / land_conflict / post_run_check_failed), move to next tier.\n- Record tier decision + escalation chain in routing event on the bead so telemetry reflects where work actually landed.\n\n### User-facing CLI\n\n- ddx work --profile default — the new primary invocation. Most users run this.\n- ddx work --profile cheap / fast / smart — opt-in deviations.\n- --max-tier continues to cap the ladder (explicit user override beats config).\n\n### Telemetry + measurement\n\nEvery execution event records: requested_profile, requested_tier, resolved_tier, escalation_count, final_tier. Aggregated metrics (FEAT-016 / ddx-4b59b816) report per-profile throughput, per-tier cost, per-bead escalation rate.\n\n## Exit gate\n\n'ddx work --profile default --once --local' against a ready bead on a host with a healthy local provider AND healthy cloud fallback:\n- Claims the bead.\n- Tries cheap tier (local). If it succeeds, closes bead with tier=cheap recorded.\n- If cheap fails for any escalation-triggering reason, automatically retries at standard tier without operator intervention.\n- If standard fails, retries at smart.\n- Final outcome recorded faithfully (success at whatever tier landed, or exhaustion if all tiers failed).\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Dynamic profile selection ('auto-pick profile based on bead complexity'). Too much magic; explicit profile choice is fine.\n- Cost budgets / circuit breakers. FEAT-014 (token awareness) tracks this separately.\n- Per-bead profile overrides (bead-level 'requires-profile: smart'). Could be a future follow-up but out of scope here.\n\n## Metadata\n\nGoverning spec: FEAT-006 (agent service) + the autorouting epic ddx-2d974641. This bead is the PROFILE-layer policy that sits on top of the routing fixes from ddx-2d974641. Do not start until ddx-2d974641's Phase-1 children are closed.",

.claude/skills/.ddx/executions/20260421T163240-eb3cb31f/prompt.md:5:## Target UX

The user-facing profile vocabulary (cheap / default / fast / smart) should map to concrete routing policies that take full advantage of local models without blocking on their limits. Specifically:

### default profile (the common case)
Try local models first, escalate to cloud if:
- local model's context window is too small for the bead prompt
- local model lacks a capability the bead requires (tools, structured output, etc.)
- local dispatch fails (provider unreachable, timeout, model-side error)
- bead reopens due to post-run check failure (review-malfunction, land-conflict, etc.)

Escalation target: next tier up in the ladder (standard cloud — e.g. codex gpt-5.4 within subscription). If THAT fails, escalate once more to smart cloud. Then stop and unclaim.

### cheap profile
Local only. Never escalate. Intended for high-volume exploratory work where cost dominates and adequate-quality is sufficient. If the cheap tier can't serve the bead, the bead unclaims and waits.

### fast profile
Cloud-first, skip local entirely. Intended for latency-critical or interactive work where local inference's 9s warmup is unacceptable. Starts at a fast cloud model (e.g. gemini-flash or haiku) and escalates to smart on failure.

### smart profile
Cloud-high-quality from the start. Intended for bead-review, adversarial checks, or beads whose AC demands high-quality reasoning. No escalation — if smart fails, the work fails meaningfully.

## Why this matters (dogfooding signal)

As of 2026-04-18, DDx's default is cheap = qwen/qwen3.6 local (vidar-omlx). With v0.3.14 shipping the SSE-comment-frame filter, the local path actually works end-to-end. The queue can drain mostly on local hardware for pennies per bead, escalating only when the bead genuinely needs cloud quality. That's the throughput-per-dollar goal (goal:throughput-per-dollar label).

## Prereqs (already filed under ddx-2d974641 or sibling beads)

This epic is blocked on the autorouting epic delivering its exit-gate UX — 'declare provider + use canonical ref + autorouting works'. Specifically:

- ddx-8610020e (RouteRequest.Provider field) — foundation
- ddx-0486e601 (fuzzy normalization + per-provider) — lets cheap-tier model refs resolve to provider-native IDs
- ddx-2f5a2284 (catalog/Profile discovery integration) — lets Profile tier overrides retarget to provider-native IDs
- ddx-3c5ba7cc (tier escalation --provider affinity) — the lower half of this epic's 'escalate on failure' semantics
- ddx-4817edfd (capability gating) — prevents routing to a model that can't serve the bead

With those in place, the profile-level policy here is mostly CONFIGURATION + a small amount of scoring-layer logic.

## Scope

### Config schema additions

.ddx/config.yaml agent.routing extends with an explicit per-profile ladder:

    agent:
      routing:
        profile_ladders:
          default: [cheap, standard, smart]      # local first, escalate
          cheap:   [cheap]                        # local only
          fast:    [fast, smart]                  # cloud fast, escalate to smart
          smart:   [smart]                        # cloud high-quality, no escalation
        model_overrides:
          cheap:    qwen/qwen3.6
          fast:     kimi/k2.5
          standard: codex/gpt-5.4                # new: subscription cloud, mid-quality
          smart:    minimax/minimax-m2.7

Current profile_priority field (a flat list) becomes a legacy alias for profile_ladders.default with a deprecation warning.

### Tier-escalation policy (routing.go)

For a given profile P, iterate profile_ladders[P]:
- Evaluate model_overrides[tier] resolves on a viable candidate (with --provider affinity from ddx-3c5ba7cc).
- If yes, try it; on failure (execution_failed / land_conflict / post_run_check_failed), move to next tier.
- Record tier decision + escalation chain in routing event on the bead so telemetry reflects where work actually landed.

### User-facing CLI

- ddx work --profile default — the new primary invocation. Most users run this.
- ddx work --profile cheap / fast / smart — opt-in deviations.
- --max-tier continues to cap the ladder (explicit user override beats config).

### Telemetry + measurement

Every execution event records: requested_profile, requested_tier, resolved_tier, escalation_count, final_tier. Aggregated metrics (FEAT-016 / ddx-4b59b816) report per-profile throughput, per-tier cost, per-bead escalation rate.

## Exit gate

'ddx work --profile default --once --local' against a ready bead on a host with a healthy local provider AND healthy cloud fallback:
- Claims the bead.
- Tries cheap tier (local). If it succeeds, closes bead with tier=cheap recorded.
- If cheap fails for any escalation-triggering reason, automatically retries at standard tier without operator intervention.
- If standard fails, retries at smart.
- Final outcome recorded faithfully (success at whatever tier landed, or exhaustion if all tiers failed).

## Out of scope

- Dynamic profile selection ('auto-pick profile based on bead complexity'). Too much magic; explicit profile choice is fine.
- Cost budgets / circuit breakers. FEAT-014 (token awareness) tracks this separately.
- Per-bead profile overrides (bead-level 'requires-profile: smart'). Could be a future follow-up but out of scope here.

## Metadata

Governing spec: FEAT-006 (agent service) + the autorouting epic ddx-2d974641. This bead is the PROFILE-layer policy that sits on top of the routing fixes from ddx-2d974641. Do not start until ddx-2d974641's Phase-1 children are closed.

.claude/skills/.ddx/executions/20260411T190840-b25e589b/manifest.json:12:    "description": "\u003ccontext-digest\u003e\nReview area: host+user ddx-server planning stack after the server model evolved beyond the earlier multi-project topology draft. Evidence covers FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, TP-002, the fresh-eyes server review, and the updated requirements for a per-user host daemon, localhost HTTP UI, SQLite-backed runtime/index state, embedded-agent progress visibility, provider dashboarding, replay-backed fixtures, and Playwright coverage for every new UI surface.\n\u003c/context-digest\u003e\nUpdate the governing server planning docs so they describe the current intended host+user ddx-server model before follow-on server design and implementation beads continue.\n\n## Goals\n- Reconcile FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, and TP-002 around a single host+user ddx-server instance with explicit project registry, worker management boundaries, and queue semantics\n- Define the split between git-backed project truth, tracked execution evidence, and host-local runtime/index state backed by an embedded database\n- Define the current transport/security model: localhost unauthenticated HTTP for UI/API now, explicit future transport/auth evolution later\n- Add the embedded-agent progress, provider dashboard, replay-backed fixture, and Playwright coverage requirements to the governed server plan\n- Make the scheduling boundary explicit: server may allocate workers across projects, but bead ordering remains owned by each project queue\n\n## Boundaries\n- Keep Axon and multi-host service-backed topology as later-stage work\n- Do not reopen the already-shipped single-project execute-bead loop contract except where the new server plan must reference it",

Evidence covers FEAT-002 server surfaces, FEAT-008 web UI, FEAT-014 token-awareness/routing metrics, the embedded-agent progress lane, and the requirement that a host+user ddx-server expose actionable provider availability and utilization state across configured harnesses/providers.\n\u003c/context-digest\u003e\nDefine the server-side agent dashboard that lets operators inspect configured providers and make informed execution decisions.\n\n## Goals\n- Show configured providers/harnesses, effective routing availability, auth/health state, and current quota or headroom when known\n- Show utilization and performance signals already normalized by DDx: token usage, cost when available, recent latency/success, burn estimates, and freshness timestamps\n- Support search/sort/filter/reporting across provider, model, profile, status, and time window\n- Distinguish provider-native facts from DDx-derived estimates and from unknown values\n\n## Required spec work\n- Update FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 so the dashboard and its backing read model are explicitly governed\n- Define the API/read-model fields exposed to UI consumers and reporting surfaces\n- Clarify how live provider availability, cached quota snapshots, and historical usage are presented without inventing certainty\n\n## Required implementation planning\n- Define sample/fixture data needed to Playwright-test the dashboard states\n- Define how the dashboard relates to worker progress, execution history, and routing diagnostics without collapsing them into one page",

Define the server-side agent dashboard that lets operators inspect configured providers and make informed execution decisions.

## Goals
- Show configured providers/harnesses, effective routing availability, auth/health state, and current quota or headroom when known
- Show utilization and performance signals already normalized by DDx: token usage, cost when available, recent latency/success, burn estimates, and freshness timestamps
- Support search/sort/filter/reporting across provider, model, profile, status, and time window
- Distinguish provider-native facts from DDx-derived estimates and from unknown values

## Required spec work
- Update FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 so the dashboard and its backing read model are explicitly governed
- Define the API/read-model fields exposed to UI consumers and reporting surfaces
- Clarify how live provider availability, cached quota snapshots, and historical usage are presented without inventing certainty

## Required implementation planning
- Define sample/fixture data needed to Playwright-test the dashboard states
- Define how the dashboard relates to worker progress, execution history, and routing diagnostics without collapsing them into one page

.ddx/beads.jsonl:265:{"acceptance":"FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 define an agent dashboard showing configured providers, availability/health, utilization metrics, freshness, and unknown-state semantics; the design includes a queryable read model and deterministic fixture scenarios for Playwright coverage","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-14T05:16:15Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1412283","closing_commit_sha":"77ac255569b6a9d82302bec4969160a538a70ca1","created_at":"2026-04-10T22:16:05.174041066Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-359706d2","depends_on_id":"ddx-ffda4fb5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-10T22:20:18Z"}],"description":"\u003ccontext-digest\u003e\nReview area: server agent observability and operator controls. Evidence covers FEAT-002 server surfaces, FEAT-008 web UI, FEAT-014 token-awareness/routing metrics, the embedded-agent progress lane, and the requirement that a host+user ddx-server expose actionable provider availability and utilization state across configured harnesses/providers.\n\u003c/context-digest\u003e\nDefine the server-side agent dashboard that lets operators inspect configured providers and make informed execution decisions.\n\n## Goals\n- Show configured providers/harnesses, effective routing availability, auth/health state, and current quota or headroom when known\n- Show utilization and performance signals already normalized by DDx: token usage, cost when available, recent latency/success, burn estimates, and freshness timestamps\n- Support search/sort/filter/reporting across provider, model, profile, status, and time window\n- Distinguish provider-native facts from DDx-derived estimates and from unknown values\n\n## Required spec work\n- Update FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 so the dashboard and its backing read model are explicitly governed\n- Define the API/read-model fields exposed to UI consumers and reporting surfaces\n- Clarify how live provider availability, cached quota snapshots, and historical usage are presented without inventing certainty\n\n## Required implementation planning\n- Define sample/fixture data needed to Playwright-test the dashboard states\n- Define how the dashboard relates to worker progress, execution history, and routing diagnostics without collapsing them into one .ddx/beads.jsonl:396:{"acceptance":"FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 define an agent dashboard showing configured providers, availability/health, utilization metrics, freshness, and unknown-state semantics; the design includes a queryable read model and deterministic fixture scenarios for Playwright coverage","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","created_at":"2026-04-10T22:14:49.010697815Z","description":"\u003ccontext-digest\u003e\nReview area: server agent observability and operator controls. Evidence covers FEAT-002 server surfaces, FEAT-008 web UI, FEAT-014 token-awareness/routing metrics, the embedded-agent progress lane, and the requirement that a host+user ddx-server expose actionable provider availability and utilization state across configured harnesses/providers.\n\u003c/context-digest\u003e\nDefine the server-side agent dashboard that lets operators inspect configured providers and make informed execution decisions.\n\n## Goals\n- Show configured providers/harnesses, effective routing availability, auth/health state, and current quota or headroom when known\n- Show utilization and performance signals already normalized by DDx: token usage, cost when available, recent latency/success, burn estimates, and freshness timestamps\n- Support search/sort/filter/reporting across provider, model, profile, status, and time window\n- Distinguish provider-native facts from DDx-derived estimates and from unknown values\n\n## Required spec work\n- Update FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 so the dashboard and its backing read model are explicitly governed\n- Define the API/read-model fields exposed to UI consumers and reporting surfaces\n- Clarify how live provider availability, cached quota snapshots, and historical usage are presented without inventing certainty\n\n## Required implementation planning\n- Define sample/fixture data needed to Playwright-test the dashboard states\n- Define how the dashboard relates to worker progress, execution history, and routing diagnostics without collapsing them into one
